result:
Mutable row:
- Simple read: SELECT balance, one row.
- Atomic decrement: UPDATE ... SET balance = balance - x WHERE balance >= x. Single-statement safety.
- Matches existing wallet code — reuse patterns, less cognitive load, shared bugs already shaken out.
- Cheap storage, cheap indexes.
- Loses history. Refunds, disputes, partial reversals reconstructed from order/payment tables — fragile if those get pruned or schema drifts.
- Audit trail lives outside the money object. Finance team asks "why is balance 7.42?" and you stitch joins.
- Concurrent writers fight same row — hot row contention if a code ever gets shared/high-traffic (promo drops, corporate cards).

Event-sourced ledger:
- Append-only. Every issue/redeem/refund/expire is row. Balance = SUM(amount) or materialized projection.
- Natural audit. Regulators, chargebacks, finance reconciliation all read same table.
- Refund semantics clean: insert compensating event, never mutate past.
- No row contention on writes — inserts scale, though SUM reads need projection/cache.
- Double-spend prevention harder: needs serializable tx, advisory lock, or balance-check-then-insert with unique constraint on (card_id, idempotency_key).
- More code. Projection staleness if you cache. Migration cost.
- Storage grows linearly with activity.

For gift cards specifically, ledger pulls ahead vs wallets because:
- Gift cards are bearer instruments — legal/accounting treats unredeemed balance as liability on balance sheet. Finance wants the ledger.
- Revenue recognition triggers on redemption events, not on balance deltas. Events are the native unit.
- Breakage (expired unused balance) accounting needs issuance date per dollar — mutable row loses that.
- Disputes/fraud investigations happen months later; history matters.

Wallets you built as mutable probably because they were internal credit with no external liability exposure. Gift cards cross that line.

Hybrid worth considering: ledger as source of truth, mutable balance row as denormalized cache updated in same tx. Get both — but now two things to keep consistent, and most bugs live in that gap.

My lean: pure ledger for gift cards even though wallets are mutable. Accept the divergence from wallet code — the domains have different audit requirements.
